tensions.text: Yeah, that's more tied to things like mental health and, like, if I'm failing exams... stuff like that.

Lunch... I try to bring some food with me if I've made anything, like meal-planned, but I'm not always super great about that. And then if I don't, then I just, like, try to go to one of the restaurants around.

Delivery? Yeah. Still like my associations... It's better for food delivery, and I've had good experiences with them in general. Like, no one's stolen my food off of it! And this thing that you told me about... the free delivery service.

I know what I like and I like what I know with cooking. In an ideal world, I would experiment a little bit more, but at the minute, I don't really have the time. And it's not something that's too important to me. Like, I really like food and I really like eating, but I'm someone that can, like, eat the same things over and over again and, like, not get bored of it.
tensions.tension: Claims to 'really like food and eating' but describes purely utilitarian relationship

tensions.text: So today, I didn't eat as much as I normally do. I actually skipped both breakfast and lunch and just had a small snack, which was a sliced avocado. That's something I normally eat, but I would eat it with other things. The reason I didn't eat that much today until the evening is because I wasn't really feeling that well until the evening, until dinner time.

tensions.text: Today, I ate, not that much. Just a banana for breakfast and then some leftover food from yesterday for lunch. I normally eat more than this, and I normally don't skip dinner. So, yeah, so I I slept quite a lot this afternoon. And when I woke up, I didn't really feel like having dinner, so it's not reflective of what I usually eat.

tensions.text: But I'd say we do it like 50, 50. For me it's 50. 50. I cook 50% of the time. I order food online 50% of the time.

tensions.text: But I'm awful at shopping, so I... I'm always forgetting to... to buy something or pick something up... I can't shop hungry. I've been on a run, and I'm shopping after that. I'd say having more... having more time might mean ordering less. I've also got less money. So, I'd say that is a big issue. It's... it is quite expensive.

So I always, almost all the time just do the prep when I'm about to cook because I never know what I'm eating the next day. So I can't really meal prep for the day ahead. So they have this Lidl wrapped like a Spotify wrapped. I think there was a month I went to the supermarket every single day. Like I've been to Lidl like 250 times last year. And that's because I can't decide on what I cook. Like what I have for the next day.
I'm definitely more of eating out person or, you know, getting takeaway person that I am a cooking person. Although I'm trying to change that this year.

tensions.text: Oh, it's like an unspoken ritual... We're not meant to call it a 'ritual,' but it definitely is.

tensions.text: A lot on TikTok, I would say... always, like, searching recipes and stuff for inspo.

tensions.text: I don't love eating the same thing over and over, so I kind of try to have ingredients that I can make in a bunch of ways because I tried the whole meal-prepping route, and then I just got so, like, redundant. I was like, I can't keep eating the same thing over and over.

It is really expensive. Like that pizza I got order ordered there for that was something ends up €32. Like I can't believe I'm spending this but at the same time it is. Yeah, it's a treat.

Health vs Convenience.tension: Major contradiction with diary showing daily home cooking

Health vs Convenience.text: I think it actually reflected my daily habit apart from the lunch because I usually have proper lunch, like, any meals or, like, with with roasted chicken or rice and all. But apart from that, that's, like, my daily routine unless I, like, order it from outside, for the dinner.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Contradicts interview claim of cooking once a week

Health vs Convenience.text: I think, yeah, that like, on a usual day, I would have lunch. I would be eating lunch, the one I have skipped today, but as it was, a long shift today for me, I've run jobs, so I didn't have my lunch, like, the second meal of my day. So that's the only thing that's, like, different from my daily routine. Otherwise, yeah, that's, like, my meal that's, like, my go to habit and, like, my usual routine. So, like, light breakfast and lunch, and then a bit of heavy and some, some good dinner to end the day. That's it.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Contradicts interview claim of cooking once a week

Health vs Convenience.text: And, like, apart from missing the lunch again, like, I know I said this before, but apart from missing the lunch, I think that's, like, my normal routine. And, yeah, of course, like, I don't eat out outside food, that much. But, yeah, it was like we were, like, too tired and, like, we were we had a plan to go somewhere out to eat Indian food, so we just went to eat it today.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Contradicts interview claim of cooking once a week

Health vs Convenience.text: Apart from that, like, my usual breakfast is like this only, just like a light breakfast with tea or coffee. And I might have lunch sometimes. Sometimes I skip it. Depends on my class and, like, my lectures. And about the dinner, mostly it's like as you may have noticed, mostly it's like home cooked meals only. But today, we try we decided to go out.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Major contradiction with interview claim of cooking once a week

Health vs Convenience.text: I had planned to cook food, but I got home late and wanted just something easy and comforting, and that was the best option.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Had planned to cook but delivery became the fallback

Health vs Convenience.text: When I'm cooking for both of us, it's all from scratch. When it's just myself... she's a whale or something. I sometimes just... lazy and have, like, a... I guess, yeah, ready meal type thing sometimes, or something from a jar or whatever. But yeah, when I'm cooking for other people, it always has to be from scratch.
Health vs Convenience.tension: 1:114-1:117 vs social cooking standards

Health vs Convenience.text: Cooking for me would be like you're actually like preparing something. So you're like, you're chopping the vegetables and you're cooking the meat and you're putting all together, you're making the sauce, that type of thing. So like you're actually putting like the ingredients together to make Something.
Health vs Convenience.tension: understands_cooking_but_avoids_it

Health vs Convenience.text: Like almost never. I only really cook if it's like I'm cooking for someone or people are coming over, which is maybe like once every two, three months or something like that.
Health vs Convenience.tension: understands_cooking_but_avoids_it

Health vs Convenience.text: Cooking means actually making something because I don't really like making quick meals. If I cook, it's something that I'm going to like and enjoy. So I wouldn't be like this type of. Because I know a lot of people like to make quick meals, but I'm not the type of person. If I'm going to cook and spend time to cook, I'll actually make a decent meal. That takes a while. Yeah, essentially it's just a nice filling meal.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Aspirational cooking identity - wants elaborate meals but often lacks energy

Health vs Convenience.text: I've realized even throughout this diary that I have done a lot of takeaways, and I think my aim will be more to cook at home more as I have today. I think today, I just felt like another craving of eating something sweet, so, I decided to make a carbonara, meal.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Realizes high takeaway usage, wants to cook more but still driven by cravings

Health vs Convenience.text: In the breakfast, I ate some fruits and yogurt and made a smoothie out of apple, carrot, and some lemon juice, because I didn't wanna eat much before, I went to the sauna. In the afternoon, I went to the sauna with my friends and then went to, two restaurants straight out and had had a pizza. Yep. Had a pizza. I shared the pizza with some of my friends. And then we went to the campus for some work. And, yeah, and then after we got back home from campus, one of my front friends called, where whether I wanted to get some food in a nearby restaurant. And I was like, yeah. Okay. So we went there, and it was an Indian place. And that is why we went there. And we were done with all the meals today.
Health vs Convenience.tension: Diary shows predominantly social eating out vs claimed 50% cooking

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Yeah, but it just encouraged me to eat way too much takeaway, so I deleted it.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Value recognition vs self-control - cancelled despite good value

Cost-Value Paradox.text: I'd say about... I don't want to lie... I'm saying about 90 to 100 quid just alone on food, maybe even more, but I'd have to check. But I definitely know it's 100 and more!
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: high spending despite claiming to be money-conscious

Cost-Value Paradox.text: So, I'd say 90%... no, maybe 80% is on the takeaways, 20% on the groceries, and then going out without including that... I'd say it's like another €40 on top of it if I'm getting drinks as well.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: massive proportion of spending on takeaways

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Maybe... maybe the delivery fees, because the shopper is kind of a bit far, and I do have the subscription, but it's around 2 to 3 Euros. That... so it kind of, like, throws me off when I know I can just walk down instead of being lazy.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: acknowledges laziness as a factor while feeling guilty about it

Cost-Value Paradox.text: No, if it goes over, then it just goes over. But I like to, like, keep it under the 30 amounts, pretty much.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: flexible budgeting despite claiming to be money-conscious

Cost-Value Paradox.text: So, I'm looking for something not too expensive... something that's medium range, that wouldn't hurt the bank or anything like that. So, yeah, that would be mostly the price... something that's not too expensive.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: claims price consciousness while spending €140/week on food

Cost-Value Paradox.text: I'm a... really, I'm really picky on money. Like, I'm a saver. Like, if I see something on promotion or reduced, I'm always buying it. Like, if I see the juices on promotion, reduced, or in a promotion, then yeah, I'm going into the shop, and I'm buying it. So yeah, I kind of like to save my money.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: claims to be money-conscious while spending €140/week on food

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Uber, unfortunately... but it wouldn't have my favorite restaurants. But, on price, then... yeah, Uber would definitely take over!
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: torn between price advantage and restaurant preference

Cost-Value Paradox.text: I would say it's a part of my normal spending, but, like, it is a treat... Like, I do really like delivery over going somewhere. But that's why it's part of my normal spending, because I think it's worth it.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Delivery is simultaneously normal spending and a treat

Cost-Value Paradox.text: It is really expensive. Like that pizza I got order ordered there for that was something ends up €32. Like I can't believe I'm spending this but at the same time it is. Yeah, it's a treat.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: cost_vs_treat_justification

Cost-Value Paradox.text: It's honestly probably if they did like a. I don't know if you've seen like Spotify wrapped where they tell you your stats for what you've listened to for the year, but if they did that for delivery, I think that would come up top every single year because it's honestly my favorite. And because, like, I'm not originally from Bristol, they don't, they don't have that like near I. Where I live at home. So it's, it's like I'm kind of just. Yeah, it's my go to. I'm a bit obsessed.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Extreme emotional attachment to Greek food contradicts rational price-focused framing

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Because I think for me, like, it's convincing me to, like. Like, I. I don't know. Like for me, I mean, it's the convenience of obviously getting the food delivered, but it's also like, I could already just make that food. So I'm like, you know, there's something else to think. Oh, well, I'm saving a bit of money. I'm not saving money at all here, but in my head I'm saving money, you know, like, I'm like, oh, well, it's free delivery. It's not as expensive as I thought it would be. You know, like, it's kind of like another push for me to just go, I'll order it. And it just makes it more like, reasonable in my head, I guess.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Acknowledges she's not actually saving money but feels like she is

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Normally, I would have sort of had a takeaway or some food out at a restaurant, but just the way this study's lined up with my week, I've ended up having quite a budget saving week.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Acknowledges this week was unusually budget-focused, implying normal weeks include more takeaways

Cost-Value Paradox.text: But yeah, I would say I would want to spend less on food and potentially, like, cook more on weekends as well. If I had to kind of, like, make an adjustment, that's what I would... I would do. But yeah, I would say probably less... less food delivery next year potentially.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: 1-448 vs cost concerns

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Delivery? I have, I think this must be included with my prime or something. So I have like Deliveroo Silver. I think I don't actually entirely know what that gets me. I do have it and I do use it every now and then, but it would mostly be if it was a restaurant that isn't on Uber. I would then check Deliveroo.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: has_subscription_but_unclear_benefits

Cost-Value Paradox.text: Not as much as I thought it would, probably. And I think that's because they say like free delivery and reduced service fees and they are like, they show you. But also sometimes even the reduced service fees, they're still adding on like £2 or whatever. So that's fine. Like it's £2 that you would have spent whether or not you had the subscription, but not as much as I thought I would or thought that it might.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: subscription_expectations_vs_reality

Cost-Value Paradox.text: But I don't really compare them. Like it's kind of like it's always delivery and if delivery doesn't have what I want then just. It is my second option. Like I wouldn't really compare. Like it would all. I probably should compare. But it kind of like delivers the first option.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: P032_INT_053 vs claims of price sensitivity

Cost-Value Paradox.text: I feel like the most stressful meals are definitely dinner. It is very time-consuming to make dinner because, with lunch, it can be something a bit more simple, like... just like a Greek yogurt bowl or something like that. But for dinner, I'm trying to get, like, everything in. So, for example, last night I think I spent, like, an hour and... like almost an hour and a half making dinner. And that's, like, stressful because I'm like, I could be getting work done right, right now! And I was like, this is pushing back my sleep as well. So that's definitely the most stressful meal of the week.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Health aspirations vs time pressure - wants nutritious meals but stressed about time cost

Cost-Value Paradox.text: I think, in terms of food, it doesn't matter to me that much. I feel like I... as a uni student, you can't be very picky with, you know, kind of like the quality of certain foods... I feel like. So that's where I'm, like, willing to even take, like, 1 or 2 pounds off and switch to the other kind.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Health-conscious identity vs budget constraints - compromises quality for price

Cost-Value Paradox.text: But I think the thing that is more important for me is certain things, like cosmetics and, like, certain, like, bath and body type of products, where I need, like, a specific kind of those things. And I think if it's, like, one or two pounds off for the off-brand, I would still stick with the more expensive one if it meant that I could get the one I wanted.
Cost-Value Paradox.tension: Price sensitivity varies by category - flexible on food, rigid on personal care

Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.text: So I didn't get, I didn't get one this evening, even though I was on my own, which I would probably usually do. But, yeah, I wasn't in the mood to cook, too much. And, yeah, this is fairly reflective of a normal kind of evening day for me.
Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.tension: Usually orders when alone but didn't this time - shows variability in solo ordering

Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.text: Weekends, I go out by myself, and I go to the place physically. It's like if I'm going for a takeaway or a restaurant, I go in myself and with the social group.
Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.tension: contrasts weekday delivery dependency with weekend social dining

Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.text: So, during weekdays, Monday to Friday, and like weekends, maybe once... but I try to avoid it on weekends because I like going out more on weekends when I have time.
Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.tension: contrasts weekday delivery dependency with weekend social preference

Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.text: I have, like, one main flatmate that I usually coordinate with, like when our lectures are, when we'll go to the gym, and so that we try to eat around the same time because we prefer not to eat, like, completely alone. But our flat is also very social, so anytime I go in the kitchen, it's almost guaranteed I'm gonna have someone to eat with. That's just my main priority, no matter who it is...
Social Pressure vs Individual Choice.tension: Strong social eating preference contradicts individual food decision-making

Dependency vs Control.text: So today was actually a different day because I was with my family at home. So most of the time, I'm always adding takeout or takeout pretty much or quick food on the ground. But this morning and also yesterday, my food was cooked at home. I did go out to a restaurant yesterday, and I did have McDonald's as well today. So my morning was breakfast done for me, which was easy. And then during lunch, also, I had my food done. And then for night, we decided to have McDonald's because I was kinda craving it.
Dependency vs Control.tension: contrasts home family eating with Dublin delivery dependency

Dependency vs Control.text: Oh, it's like an unspoken ritual... We're not meant to call it a 'ritual,' but it definitely is.
Dependency vs Control.tension: P001_INT_016 vs family awareness of dependency
